plainMeaning: Here the reciter owns up to all the ways they have failed in living their faith — careless temple worship, disrespect toward the teacher, mishandling of sacred images and temple funds — and to all the ways they have skipped or spoiled their fasts and self-imposed restraints.
recitation: 2. tathā darśanācāre samyaktva virādhiuṁ hoya. deva, guru namaskāra karyā pākhe bhojana kīdhuṁ hoya. deva pūjatāṁ dhotī śuddha kīdhī na hoya. mukhakośa kīdho na hoya. ādhaḍaviche deva pūjyā hoya. gurune āsaṇe, besaṇe, upagaraṇe paga lagāḍyo hoya, guruvacana dveṣalage vālyuṁ hoya. biṁba hāthathakī vichuṭyuṁ hoya, biṁbanuṁ vecāvaccha kīdhuṁ na hoya, devadravya āpaṇe vyavasāye ghālyuṁ hoya. devadravya viṇasatuṁ uvekhyuṁ hoya. aneruṁ e darśanācāra viṣe. ॥ 2 ॥ 3. tapācāre chatī śakte paccakkhāṇa kīdhuṁ na hoya, karīne bhāṅgyuṁ hoya, paccakkhāṇa pālyuṁ na hoya, niyama, abhigraha leï bhāṅgyā hoya, cha bheda abhyantara, cha bheda bāhya tapa avasare sācavyuṁ na hoya. aneruṁ e tapācāra viṣe. ॥ 3 ॥

wordByWord.gloss: second (section) — the confession of darśanācāra, conduct-in-faith
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ordinal marking the second of the five-ācāra confessions (jñāna being the first, in verse 15)
wordByWord.notes: Numbers the ācāra, not a vow; the five-ācāra tally follows the twelve vows.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit tathā 'thus, so'; here a connective opening each ācāra clause
wordByWord.notes: Links this section to the preceding jñānācāra confession.

wordByWord.gloss: in (the matter of) darśanācāra — right conduct regarding faith
wordByWord.etymology: darśana (faith/right view) + ācāra (conduct), locative -e; cf. preface gloss of ācāra
wordByWord.notes: Distinct from the five samyaktva-atichāras confessed earlier; this is faith enacted in worship and reverence.

wordByWord.etymology: samyak ('right') + abstract -tva; defined in the preface glossary
Prakrit-Gujarati from Sanskrit vi-rādh- 'to offend, mar'; opposite of ārādhanā (devout fulfilment)
wordByWord.notes: virādhanā = the marring of an observance; the master verb governing this whole section.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati optative of hovuṁ 'to be', from Sanskrit bhū-
wordByWord.notes: The optative auxiliary that closes every clause of the confession ('… may have been done'); it recurs after each listed lapse throughout the verse.

wordByWord.gloss: the Deva — the Jina / Tīrthaṅkara, the object of worship
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit deva 'divine one'; in Jain usage the Vītarāga Jina
wordByWord.notes: Recurs several times below (deva pūjatāṁ, deva pūjyā).

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit guru 'weighty, venerable'
wordByWord.notes: Paired with deva in the formula deva-guru-namaskāra.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit namas ('homage') + kāra ('making')
Gujarati past participle of karavuṁ, from Sanskrit kṛ-
Old Gujarati pākhe 'apart from, without' (cf. pakṣa 'side')
wordByWord.notes: 'namaskāra karyā pākhe bhojana' = eaten before paying obeisance to Deva and Guru.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit bhojana 'eating, food', from bhuj-
Old Gujarati kīdhuṁ, past participle of karavuṁ; variant of karyuṁ
wordByWord.notes: Neuter form agreeing with bhojana, paccakkhāṇa, etc.; recurs with na hoya = 'may not have done'.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati present participle of pūjavuṁ, from Sanskrit pūj-
wordByWord.notes: 'deva pūjatāṁ' = at the time of worshipping the Jina.

wordByWord.etymology: from Sanskrit dhauta 'washed'; the unstitched cloth worn during temple worship
wordByWord.notes: Here the garment itself, not the act of washing; the lapse is failing to keep it ritually pure.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit śuddha, from śudh- 'to purify'
feminine form of kīdhuṁ, agreeing with dhotī (fem.)
Sanskrit/Prakrit na, negation
wordByWord.notes: Recurs repeatedly; 'na hoya' = 'may NOT have been (done)' — confessing omissions as well as wrong acts.

wordByWord.gloss: the mouth-cover (cloth over nose and mouth during pūjā)
wordByWord.etymology: mukha ('mouth') + kośa ('sheath, cover')
wordByWord.notes: Worn so that breath does not defile the image; failing to wear it is the lapse. Cf. the muhapatti, but here the worship-time mouth-cloth.

wordByWord.etymology: masculine form of kīdhuṁ, agreeing with mukhakośa (masc.)
Old Gujarati colloquial; 'in a slapdash, distracted manner' (ā-dhaḍ-vice)
wordByWord.notes: Reading uncertain; sense is worshipping the Jina inattentively or in a rush. Flagged in notes.

wordByWord.etymology: past participle of pūjavuṁ, Sanskrit pūj-
wordByWord.notes: 'ādhaḍaviche deva pūjyā hoya' = the Jina may have been worshipped carelessly.

wordByWord.etymology: guru + dative-genitive -ne
wordByWord.notes: Governs the following list of the guru's things touched by the foot.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit āsana 'seat', locative -e
Gujarati besaṇuṁ 'seat', from besavuṁ 'to sit'
wordByWord.notes: Near-synonym pairing with āsaṇe, in the liturgical doublet style.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit upakaraṇa 'implement'; the mendicant's permitted possessions
wordByWord.notes: Touching any of the guru's seat/requisites with the foot is irreverent — the confessed fault.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati paga, from Sanskrit pada
causative of lāgavuṁ 'to touch', from Sanskrit lag-
wordByWord.notes: 'paga lagāḍyo' = touched with the foot — a gesture of disrespect.

wordByWord.etymology: guru + vacana ('speech, command')
dveṣa ('aversion') + lage ('owing to, on account of')
wordByWord.notes: The motive that makes refusing the guru's word a darśana-fault.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati vāḷavuṁ 'to turn, ward off'
wordByWord.notes: 'guruvacana dveṣalage vālyuṁ' = spurned the guru's instruction out of spite.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit bimba 'image, idol'
wordByWord.notes: Recurs as biṁbanuṁ below.

wordByWord.etymology: hātha ('hand') + thakī (ablative postposition 'from')
Gujarati vichuṭvuṁ 'to slip away, come loose'
wordByWord.notes: 'biṁba hāthathakī vichuṭyuṁ' = the image may have slipped from the hand (during handling) — a careless mishap confessed.

wordByWord.etymology: biṁba + genitive -nuṁ
Prakrit veyāvacca, Sanskrit vaiyāvṛtya 'service, ministering'
wordByWord.notes: Not 'selling'; here = failing to provide due service/maintenance to the sacred image.

wordByWord.gloss: temple property / wealth dedicated to the Deva
wordByWord.etymology: deva ('Jina') + dravya ('property, substance')
wordByWord.notes: Funds and goods consecrated to the temple; recurs in the next clause.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati āpaṇe 'we / one's own'
wordByWord.notes: 'āpaṇe vyavasāye' = into one's own business.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit vyavasāya 'occupation, enterprise', locative -e
wordByWord.notes: Diverting temple wealth into private commerce — a grave darśana-fault.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ghālavuṁ 'to put, insert'
wordByWord.notes: 'devadravya āpaṇe vyavasāye ghālyuṁ' = put temple-wealth into one's own business.

wordByWord.etymology: present participle of viṇasavuṁ, Sanskrit vi-naś- 'to perish'
Prakrit uvekkha-, Sanskrit upekṣ- 'to overlook'
wordByWord.notes: 'devadravya viṇasatuṁ uvekhyuṁ' = stood by while temple-wealth was wasted.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati aneruṁ 'other, additional', from Sanskrit anya
wordByWord.notes: The sweep-up word: 'whatever else …' — gathering unnamed lapses (cf. preface's recurring formula).

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati demonstrative e
wordByWord.notes: 'aneruṁ e darśanācāra viṣe' = whatever else (was done) regarding this darśanācāra.

wordByWord.etymology: as above, in the closing summary phrase
Sanskrit viṣaye 'in the sphere of'
wordByWord.notes: Seals the section: 'concerning this darśanācāra' / 'concerning this tapācāra'.

wordByWord.etymology: daṇḍa bars enclosing the section number, per the book's orthography
wordByWord.notes: Spaced bars are part of the printed text.

wordByWord.gloss: third (section) — the confession of tapācāra, conduct-in-austerity
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ordinal marking the third of the five-ācāra confessions

wordByWord.gloss: in (the matter of) tapācāra — right conduct regarding austerity
wordByWord.etymology: tapas ('austerity') + ācāra ('conduct'), locative -e
Gujarati chatuṁ 'existing, present', from Sanskrit san-
wordByWord.notes: 'chatī śakte' = while the capacity was actually present — i.e. no genuine excuse.

wordByWord.gloss: with the strength / capacity (to do it)
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit śakti 'power, ability', locative -e
wordByWord.notes: The phrase sharpens the fault: failing to fast though able to.

wordByWord.gloss: pratyākhyāna — a renunciation-vow (e.g. a fast or food-restriction)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit paccakkhāṇa, Sanskrit pratyākhyāna 'renunciation'; cf. the 6th Āvaśyaka
wordByWord.notes: Book spelling પચ્ચખ્ખાણ (doubled), preserved verbatim; recurs below.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati conjunctive participle of karavuṁ, 'having done'
wordByWord.notes: 'karīne bhāṅgyuṁ' = having taken the vow, then broke it.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati bhāṅgavuṁ 'to break', from Sanskrit bhañj-
wordByWord.notes: Neuter, agreeing with paccakkhāṇa.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati pāḷavuṁ 'to keep, observe', from Sanskrit pālay-
wordByWord.notes: 'paccakkhāṇa pālyuṁ na hoya' = may not have kept the vow (to its full completion).

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit niyama 'restraint, fixed observance'
Sanskrit abhigraha 'firm resolve'; a conditional vow (as in Mahāvīra's famous abhigraha)
wordByWord.notes: Paired with niyama as voluntary self-bindings whose breach is confessed.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati conjunctive participle of levuṁ 'to take', from Sanskrit lā-/grah-
as bhāṅgyuṁ above; masc./plural form agreeing with niyama, abhigraha
wordByWord.notes: 'niyama abhigraha leï bhāṅgyā' = took on rules and resolves, then broke them.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit ṣaṣ 'six'
wordByWord.notes: Recurs: six internal + six external austerities (the twelvefold tapa).

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit bheda 'division, type'
wordByWord.notes: 'cha bheda' = the six types.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit abhyantara 'inner'
wordByWord.notes: The six inner tapas: prāyaścitta, vinaya, vaiyāvṛtya, svādhyāya, vyutsarga (kāyotsarga), dhyāna.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit bāhya 'outer'
wordByWord.notes: The six outer tapas: anaśana, ūnodarī, vṛtti-saṅkṣepa, rasa-parityāga, kāya-kleśa, saṁlīnatā/vivikta-śayyāsana.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit tapas 'heat, ascetic discipline', from tap-
wordByWord.notes: The twelvefold tapa as a whole.

wordByWord.gloss: at the proper occasion / when due
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit avasara 'occasion', locative -e
Gujarati sācavavuṁ 'to keep, tend'
wordByWord.notes: 'tapa avasare sācavyuṁ na hoya' = may not have observed the (twelvefold) austerity when the occasion called for it.

wordByWord.etymology: as above, in the closing summary phrase 'aneruṁ e tapācāra viṣe'
wordByWord.notes: aneruṁ, e and viṣe recur here exactly as in section 2; see their entries above.

wordByWord.etymology: daṇḍa bars enclosing the section number

literalTranslation: And likewise, in darśanācāra (faith-conduct), right faith may have been marred: a meal may have been eaten without first saluting the Deva and the Guru; while worshipping the Jina the dhotī may not have been kept pure; the mouth-cover may not have been worn; the Jina may have been worshipped carelessly; the foot may have touched the Guru's seat, sitting-place, or requisites; the Guru's word may have been spurned out of ill-will; the sacred image may have slipped from the hand; due service to the image may not have been rendered; temple-wealth may have been put into one's own business; temple-wealth, while going to ruin, may have been ignored. Whatever else there may be concerning this darśanācāra. ॥2॥ In tapācāra (austerity-conduct): though the capacity was present, a renunciation-vow may not have been undertaken; having undertaken it, it may have been broken; the vow may not have been carried through; rules and resolves once taken may have been broken; the six inner and six outer kinds of austerity may not have been observed when due. Whatever else there may be concerning this tapācāra. ॥3॥
idiomaticTranslation: And so, in the conduct of my faith, I confess every way I have marred right faith: that I sat to eat before bowing to the Jina and my teacher; that I let my pūjā-cloth go impure and left off the mouth-cover; that I worshipped the Lord with a wandering, half-hearted mind; that my foot brushed my teacher's seat or his sacred things; that out of spite I turned away his word; that the holy image slipped careless from my hand, that I withheld the care it was owed, that I funnelled temple wealth into my own trade and stood by idle while it was squandered — and whatever else I have done amiss in faith. And in the conduct of austerity I confess: that, fully able, I took no vow of renunciation, or took one and broke it, or never saw it through; that I bound myself with rules and resolves and then let them snap; that when the hour for penance came I kept neither the six inner nor the six outer austerities — and whatever else I have failed in austerity.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After confessing lapses of jñānācāra (knowledge-conduct) in the previous verse, the reciter now moves through two more of the five ācāras: darśanācāra and tapācāra. It is vital not to confuse this darśanācāra catalogue with the five samyaktva-atichāras (śaṅkā, kāṅkṣā, …) confessed far earlier under the first vow: there faith was tested as inner conviction; here it is faith made visible in the temple and before the teacher. The lapses are deliberately concrete and bodily — eating before namaskāra, an impure dhotī, a forgotten mukhakośa, a distracted (ādhaḍaviche) pūjā, a foot carelessly touching the guru's āsana or upakaraṇa, the guru's word rebuffed dveṣa-lage (out of aversion), an image slipping from the hand. The section then rises to its gravest charges, around devadravya: diverting consecrated temple-wealth into private vyavasāya (business), and the sin of omission — watching it 'go to ruin and looking away' (viṇasatuṁ uvekhyuṁ). Both active misuse and passive neglect are owned. The tapācāra section is tighter, built on the recurring phrase chatī śakte — 'though the strength was there.' The fault is not inability but the squandering of ability: a paccakkhāṇa never taken, or taken and broken (karīne bhāṅgyuṁ), or begun and not carried to completion (pālyuṁ na); niyama and abhigraha snapped; and the twelvefold tapa — cha bheda abhyantara, cha bheda bāhya — left unobserved 'when the occasion called.' Each section closes in the sūtra's standard manner with aneruṁ … viṣe, sweeping up the unnamed remainder before the eventual micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the pañcācāra — the five disciplines of jñāna, darśana, cāritra, tapa and vīrya — the practitioner's behavioural counterpart to the ratnatraya (right faith, knowledge, conduct). Darśanācāra here is bhakti enacted: the eightfold darśanācāra of classical lists (niḥśaṅkitā, etc.) is translated into temple-floor specifics, and the devadravya clauses encode the doctrine that wealth consecrated to the Jina is inviolable — its private use or negligent loss incurs heavy karmic debt, an idea elaborated across the śrāvakācāra literature. The tapācāra section is a compact index to Tattvārtha-sūtra 9.19–20: the twelvefold austerity, six bāhya and six abhyantara, with prāyaścitta (of which this very pratikramaṇa is a form) heading the inner six. The leitmotif chatī śakte sharpens the Jain distinction between inability (no fault) and capacity unused (fault): tapa is owed in proportion to one's strength, and to under-spend that strength is itself a transgression — the seed of the vīryācāra confession that follows.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Read today, the verse asks for a two-part audit. First, of devotion-in-action: did I rush or skip obeisance, treat ritual purity casually, handle images and especially temple funds carelessly or self-servingly, or — just as culpable — stay silent while something sacred was neglected? The verse insists that looking away (uvekhyuṁ) is a confessed fault, not neutrality. Second, of effort: it is not enough to have done no harm; the reciter must ask whether, being able, they actually undertook and completed their fasts, rules and resolves. 'I was busy' does not survive chatī śakte. The practical takeaway is integrity in small ritual acts and honest follow-through on self-imposed disciplines.

crossReferences.text: Pañcācāra of jñāna-darśana-cāritra-tapa-vīrya in the śrāvakācāra tradition

sources.title: Pratikramana (overview of the rite and its confession of transgressions)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the five-ācāra confession's place within the fourth Āvaśyaka and the meaning of atichāra.

sources.consultedFor: The classical twelvefold tapa (six bāhya + six abhyantara) and the framework of vows and their atichāras.

sources.consultedFor: Cross-checking the darśanācāra worship-faults (dhotī, mukhakośa, devadravya) and the tapācāra enumeration in traditional atichāra texts.

sources.consultedFor: The verbatim Gujarati atichāra confession and the standard reading of devadravya-vyavasāya and veyāvacca (vaiyāvṛtya) of the image.

notes: Verse 16 carries sections (2) darśanācāra and (3) tapācāra of the five-ācāra confession that follows the twelve vows; it must NOT be read as the samyaktva-atichāra list given earlier under the first vow. Two readings are genuinely uncertain: (a) 'આધડવિચે' (ādhaḍaviche) is a colloquial Old-Gujarati adverb rendered here as 'hastily/half-attentively'; the exact form is not securely attested in dictionaries and the gloss is contextual. (b) 'વેચાવચ્ચ' is taken as veyāvacca = Sanskrit vaiyāvṛtya, 'service/upkeep of the image' (so 'biṁbanuṁ vecāvaccha kīdhuṁ na hoya' = failed to maintain the image), NOT as Gujarati vecavuṁ 'to sell'; some popular glosses read 'selling', but the vaiyāvṛtya sense fits the syntax (kīdhuṁ na hoya = a duty left undone) and the darśanācāra theme of caring for the consecrated image. Book orthography preserved verbatim: પચ્ચખ્ખાણ (doubled conjunct), અતિયાર-tradition spelling, and the spaced bars ॥ ૨ ॥ / ॥ ૩ ॥. The recurring auxiliary 'હોય' and negator 'ન' are glossed once and noted as recurring rather than re-listed at every occurrence, for readability, while remaining fully accounted for.
